Admission Date:  1985-03-18              Discharge Date:   2000-07-28

Date of Birth:  2019-03-07             Sex:   M

Service: SURGERY

Allergies:
Patient recorded as having No Known Allergies to Drugs

Attending:Denise
Chief Complaint:
Nausea and Vomiting

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
Extended Adhesiolysis
Cholecystectomy
PICC


History of Present Illness:
This is a 58 year old male with HIV (CD4=165 1975-10-09, VL=6,640
1994-01-14) with recent admission to hospital. He was admitted to
Cassia Regional Medical Center Hosp. on 06-22 with hematemesis, and nausea. He was
diagnosed witha small bowel obstruction and CT scan showed
obstructed Gall bladder. He underwent biliary drainage for
decompression. His hospitalizationwas complicated by aspiration
PNA (RLL) and subsequently intubated. also ATN with Cr of 7.7.
He was very agitated and required heavy sedation. He was also
trreated for COPD exacerbation with high-dose steroids. He was
then transfered to St Cloud Hospital on 1992-10-01 and remained in house until
06-16. His percutaneous [**Month/Day (4) 19843**] fell out. His PNA resolved
clinically and radiographically and his renal function returned
to normal. He saw Dr. Lynch for f/u on 09-13 and he was started
on new HIV regimen. He saw Dr. Morales on 04-10 for f/u for his
cholecystitis. He was not having symptoms of cholecystits at
that time and the plan was for a HIDA scan with close follow-up.
Since discharge he has had intermittent periods of vomiting, and
"sour stomach". He has not had a bowel movement in 2 days and
his sister, a nurse, noted no bowel sounds.

Past Medical History:
Hep C
HIV
HTN
Substance abuse (IVDU, EtOH, cocaine/heroin)
Diverticulitis s/p resection '[**50**] and temporary colostomy
reversed in '[**51**].
Hypoplastic Kidney

Social History:
Former IV heroin/cocaine user in remote hx.  + Tobacco, denies
alcohol abuse.
Lives with partner

PORTABLE ABDOMEN  1985-03-18 5:01 PM
ABDOMEN, SINGLE SUPINE PORTABLE VIEW: A nasogastric tube
terminates in the stomach. Gas is distributed throughout the
colon which is normal in caliber. In the left mid abdomen, there
are two loops of bowel which appear to be small bowel which, if
so, are dilated up to 4.8 cm in caliber. The wall of these two
loops is not well defined and there is suggestion of possible
pneumatosis intestinalis. There is no definite evidence of
pneumoperitoneum. The bones are unremarkable.

IMPRESSION: Two loops of dilated small bowel in the left mid
abdomen with suggestion of possible pneumatosis intestinalis.
Follow up radiographs or evaluation with CT are recommended.
.

UNILAT UP EXT VEINS US LEFT  1994-01-07 7:48 PM
UNILAT UP EXT VEINS US LEFT
Reason: cause of swelling in LUE below PICC placement
IMPRESSION: Nonocclusive thrombus along the PICC catheter within
the left brachial vein.
.
CT CHEST W/CONTRAST  2006-08-13 10:25 AM
IMPRESSION:
1. Dilated loops of small bowel without evidence of complete
obstruction and contrast passing through all the way to the
rectum. Findings most likely represent an ileus, though partial
or early obstruction are also diagnostic considerations.
Moderate intraabdominal and intrapelvic free fluid.
2. Scattered foci of "tree in budding" within the lung
bilaterally, worrisome for acute infection. Diagnostic
considerations include opportunistic infections in
immunocompromised patients and clinical correlation is
recommended.
3. More nodule-like pulmonary densities measure up to 6 mm and
while these may also be related to the acute infection,
follow-up to resolution is recommended.
4. Bilateral small to moderate pleural effusions.
5. Cholelithiasis.

.
UNILAT UP EXT VEINS US LEFT  2006-08-13 9:32 AM
IMPRESSION: Nonocclusive residual thrombus within the left
brachial vein.
.
UNILAT UP EXT VEINS US RIGHT  1983-09-23 7:41 PM
IMPRESSION:
Short segment occluding thrombus in the basilic vein surrounding
PICC line at the insertion site. Cephalic vein is not visualized
.

Procedure date     Tissue received     Report Date    Diagnosed
by

DIAGNOSIS:
Gallbladder:
1.  Cholelithiasis, mixed type.
2.  Chronic cholecystitis.

.


Brief Hospital Course:
This is a 58 year old male with who presented with nausea and
vomiting.
A KUB showed Two loops of dilated small bowel in the left mid
abdomen with suggestion of possible pneumatosis intestinalis. A
CXR showed worsening of right lower lung opacity and new small
right pleural effusion concerning for worsening infection.

Small Bowel Obstruction: a NGT was placed and serial exams
performed. He improved with conservative management. and bowel
rest.
A PICC and TPN were initiated on HD 4.
He reported +flatus and +BM on HD 4. The NGT was removed on HD 5
and he remained NPO, although we suspect he was sneaking fluids.

We advanced his diet on HD 6  and he was tolerating clears to
fulls.

A CT was done on 12-13 and showed dilated loops of small bowel
without evidence of complete obstruction and contrast passing
through all the way to the rectum. Findings most likely
represent an ileus, though partial or early obstruction are also
diagnostic considerations. Moderate intraabdominal and
intrapelvic free fluid. It also showed Cholelithiasis.

Pneumonia: He was started on Levo to cover ? aspiration LLL. He
was reporting no pulmonary symptoms.
A Chest CT done on 12-13 showed scattered foci of "tree in
budding" within the lung bilaterally, worrisome for acute
infection. Diagnostic considerations include opportunistic
infections in immunocompromised patients and clinical
correlation is recommended. More nodule-like pulmonary densities
measure up to 6 mm and while these may also be related to the
acute infection, follow-up to resolution is recommended. He was
started on  Bactrim DS daily for PCP [**Name9 (PRE) **] when taking PO.
Levofloxacin was stopped on 1983-09-23.

Thrombus: A non-occlusive thrombus of L brachial vein secondary
to PICC line placed 01-08. A Heparin gtt was started.
An US on 12-13 showed Nonocclusive residual thrombus within the
left brachial vein. Vascular was consulted and said
anticoagulation for 1 month. He had a new PICC placed on the
Right side and also developed a thrombus with an US showing a
short segment occluding thrombus in the basilic vein surrounding
PICC line at the insertion site.
The Heparin was stopped prior to the OR and then Lovenox was
started and discharged on Coumadin for 1 month.  His INR on day
of discharge was 1.7. He was receiving between 1 and 2 mg of
Coumadin daily and will continued follow-up.

He then went to the OR on 09-17 for CCY/lysis of adhesions.

Post-operatively he did well.

GI/Abd: He was NPO with IVF and a NGT. The NGT was removed on
POD 5. His diet was slowly advanced starting with sips on POD 6.
He reported +flatus on POD 6. We moved him along and he was
tolerating a regular diet at time of discharge.

Pain: His pain was well controlled with a PCA. Once on a diet we
were able to switch him to his home doses of PO pain meds.

C.diff: He was having loose stool and started on Flagyl for
C.diff. He will continue on Flagyl for 2 weeks.

ID: He was followed by ID for management of his HIV medications.
Once back on PO's he was restarted on Bactrim and ARVs. He also
continued on Flagyl for the C.diff. He will follow-up with Dr.
Pena on 1989-12-02.

Medications on Admission:
Bactrim, norvir, truvada, nifedine, reyotes, zantzol, oxycodone,
oxycontin

Non-occlusive thrombus of L brachial vein secondary to PICC line

Right pleural effusion

Discharge Condition:
Good
Tolerating Diet
Pain well controlled.

Discharge Instructions:
Please call your doctor or return to the ER for any of the
following:
* You experience new chest pain, pressure, squeezing or
tightness.
* New or worsening cough or wheezing.
* If you are vomitting and cannot keep in fluids or your
medications.
* You are getting dehydrated due to continued vomitting,
diarrhea or other reasons. Signs of dehydration include dry
mouth, rapid heartbeat or feeling dizzy or faint when standing.
* You see blood or dark/black material when you vomit or have a
bowel movement.
* Your skin, or the whites of your eyes become yellow.
* Your pain is not improving within 8-12 hours or not gone
within 24 hours. Call or return immediately if your pain is
getting worse or is changing location or moving to your chest or
back.
* You have shaking chills, or a fever greater than 101.5 (F)
degrees or 38(C) degrees.
* Any serious change in your symptoms, or any new symptoms that
concern you.
* Please resume all regular home medications and take any new
meds
as ordered.
* Continue to amubulate several times per day.
